naming_inspiration.description: Named for his dead half-brother Alexander Allin Hooker (b.1763; d.1781; FAG 22532503), son of Capt. James Hooker's second wife Hannah Allin — a memorial naming that also honors the Allin family name carried through Capt. James's second wife.

military_service.unit: Unknown — service documented in son's obituary
military_service.notes: Fought at Battle of Lundy's Lane (July 25, 1814) and Battle of Queenston Heights (Oct 13, 1812). Documented in obituary of his son Capt. James Henry Hooker, Rochester newspaper, March 1907: 'His father, Alexander B. Hooker, served in the war of 1812, fighting in the battles of Lundy's Lane, Queenstown, and others.'

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery — land he deeded to Irondequoit in 1840; family plot survived 1950s accidental destruction

narrative_blocks.header: Pioneer settler who gave Irondequoit its Hooker Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Alexander Allin Hooker arrived in the Rochester area in 1816 — with his brother Horace — to visit their sister Dolly (Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong, who had recently married and settled in Canandaigua) and stayed to open a general store that lasted three years; by 1828 he had purchased Jesse Tainter's farm and by 1839 he was Irondequoit's first Town Clerk, and in 1840 he deeded land that became Hooker Cemetery to the town. His family burial plot there is one of the few in the cemetery to survive intact when the town accidentally destroyed most of the grounds in the 1950s, leaving his stone among the more legible remnants of the family that gave Irondequoit its first civic institutions.

narrative_blocks.header: Fought at Lundy's Lane before founding Irondequoit
narrative_blocks.body: Before settling in the Rochester area, Alexander Allin Hooker served in the War of 1812, fighting at the battles of Lundy's Lane (July 25, 1814, one of the bloodiest engagements on the Canadian border) and Queenston Heights — service documented in his son's 1907 newspaper obituary, which called him 'a soldier' who 'served in the war of 1812, fighting in the battles of Lundy's Lane, Queenstown, and others.' He came to Rochester in 1816, the year after the war ended, and within two decades had deeded a cemetery and founded a town — a veteran who became a civic patriarch.

narrative_blocks.header: An old Indian trail passed behind his cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: The burying ground Alexander Hooker made public in 1840 sat on a sandy knoll traversed by an Old Indian Trail that ran from the Seneca Village on Boughton Hill (near Victor, New York) to the Indian Landing at the mouth of the Genesee River — meaning the Hooker Cemetery was not merely a settler burial ground but sat astride an ancient Seneca thoroughfare, and likely contains Seneca burials alongside those of the earliest European settlers. It is estimated that perhaps a thousand people are buried in the one-and-a-half acres, most without markers, interred by pioneers under pine and oak trees before the ground was formally enclosed.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her mother Lucy Case Hooker.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.title: Doctor
bio.photo_notes: Doctor Robert Morris (1809-1904), surgeon of the 91st NY Infantry

education.notes: One of the preeminent medical schools in New York.

career.notes: Retired at age 94. Most of professional life in Ogdensburg, but practiced partnership periods.

military_service.notes: Severely injured at Key West FL when thrown from horse while stationed there.

narrative_blocks.header: Translating Latin from memory at age ninety-five
narrative_blocks.body: Two months before his death in November 1904, confined to his bed in Ogdensburg, Dr. Robert Morris sent for a volume from his library and read from it aloud, translating the classical Latin text 'with a readiness and correctness that were most remarkable in one so old,' according to the 1905 tribute published in the Albany Medical Annals. His mind held perfectly at the end of a life that had crossed from Scotland to America, from a Hamilton County farm to a medical college, and from a Civil War field hospital to six decades of practice in the same St. Lawrence River city.

narrative_blocks.header: Thrown from his horse while serving as surgeon
narrative_blocks.body: As surgeon of the 91st New York Infantry, Dr. Robert Morris was 'quite severely injured while stationed at Key West, Florida, by being thrown from his horse' — a wound that did not curtail his career but rather introduced a note of battlefield contingency into an otherwise steady civilian practice. He had trained at Albany Medical College, graduating in 1846, and the Civil War interruption was the one major departure from the Ogdensburg practice he would otherwise sustain for nearly sixty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Retired at ninety-four; his practice never left Ogdensburg
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Robert Morris retired from his Ogdensburg practice in 1903 at the age of ninety-four — one of the longest documented medical careers in northern New York — and died the following year, having spent the better part of six decades within a single city on the St. Lawrence River. His wife Lucy Case Hooker Morris died in 1856 at thirty-five; he outlived her for forty-eight years, longer than she had lived.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Scotland; a surgeon six decades in Ogdensburg
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Morris was born in Scotland in 1809 and came to America as a child, his parents settling as farmers in Hammond, New York — a passage from the Scottish countryside to the St. Lawrence frontier that would have been unremarkable to the thousands of Scots who made that journey in the Napoleonic era, except that this particular boy would end up training at Albany Medical College, marrying into the founding family of Irondequoit's Hooker Cemetery, and practicing medicine until he was ninety-four. He entered the Hooker orbit entirely by geography and profession, but left a record that belongs, through his marriage to Lucy Case Hooker Morris, to the Hooker family's documentation of itself.

sources.label: Albany Medical Annals — Tribute to Dr. Robert Morris (1905), Dr. J. Montgomery Mosher, ed.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother died when she was three
narrative_blocks.body: Mary C. Morris lost her mother Lucy Case Hooker Morris in October 1856 when she was barely three years old, and grew up in Ogdensburg under the care of her father, Doctor Robert Morris — the Civil War surgeon who would practice medicine until age ninety-four. She outlived her father by thirty-three years and her mother by eighty-one, dying in 1937 at age eighty-three, her life a quiet arc from the Irondequoit frontier her grandfather Alexander Allin Hooker had helped settle to a twentieth-century death far from the Hooker Cemetery he had deeded to the town.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery Irondequoit in the family plot

narrative_blocks.header: Died at eighteen, buried in her grandfather's cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Julia H. Benjamin died on February 28, 1864 at age eighteen — the same winter Ulysses Grant was appointed lieutenant general and the Civil War entered its brutal final phase — and was buried in Hooker Cemetery in Irondequoit, the land her maternal grandfather Alexander Allin Hooker had deeded to the town in 1840. She rests alongside her mother, her grandfather, and other Hooker ancestors in the family plot that survived the accidental 1950s bulldozing that destroyed most of the surrounding grounds.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors his mother Louisa Hooker's maiden name.

Middle name 'Kendrick' almost certainly honors Rev. Asahel Clark Kendrick (I00943) — inaugural professor of Greek at the University of Rochester — who in 1857 married Whitham's aunt Helen MacNaughton Hooker (H02025), a sister of his mother Louisa Hooker. Born in 1863, six years after that marriage.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester — buried with husband Asahel Clark Kendrick

narrative_blocks.header: Died at fifty-three, survived by her professor husband
narrative_blocks.body: Helen MacNaughton Hooker Kendrick died on November 1, 1880 at age fifty-three — exactly fifteen years before her husband, Professor Asahel Clark Kendrick, died in the same Rochester home at age eighty-five after sixty years of teaching Greek at the university he had helped found in 1850. She and Kendrick were buried together at Mount Hope Cemetery, where he joined her in 1895, the dormitory that would bear his name not to be dedicated for another eighteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Her marriage linked Hartford's Puritans to Rochester's founders
narrative_blocks.body: When Helen MacNaughton Hooker married Asahel Clark Kendrick in 1857, she connected the descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker of Hartford — the founding Puritan of Connecticut — to the man who had helped establish the University of Rochester seven years earlier and would teach Greek there for thirty-eight years. The union meant that two of Kendrick's children by Helen (Ryland and Josephine) were both Hooker descendants and heirs to a Rochester academic legacy, a thread that would run through four generations to Ann Kendrick McNabb in the twenty-first century.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Reverend Asahel Clark Kendrick, DD LL.D. (1809-1895), inaugural professor of Greek at University of Rochester

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester per FA — alongside H02025 Helen MacNaughton Hooker Kendrick

notable.notable_blurb: First professor of Greek at University of Rochester

education.notes: First American professor to study at the University of Athens.

career.role: Professor of Ancient and Modern Languages
career.organization: Madison University (now Colgate University), Hamilton, New York
career.notes: Also designed the university seal in 1846.

career.role: Inaugural Professor of Greek Language and Literature (Munro Professorship)
career.notes: Also: Chair of Classics 1850-95; First Librarian 1850-66; Acting President 1863 and 1877-78; Acting Professor of Biblical Literature & NT 1865-68 and 1875-77.

career.role: Acting Professor of Biblical Literature and New Testament
career.notes: Concurrent with U of R duties.

career.role: Member, American New Testament Revision Committee
career.notes: Contributed to the Revised Version of 1881.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated Hamilton College 1831; immediately joined faculty as tutor in classics.
Inaugural Munro Professor of Greek 1850-1888; first librarian 1850-1866; acting president 1863, 1877-78. Kendrick Hall named in his honor, 1913.

narrative_blocks.header: First American professor to study Greek in Athens
narrative_blocks.body: In 1852, Asahel Clark Kendrick traveled to Greece and attended lectures at the University of Athens, becoming among the first American professors to study in the country whose language he had spent his career teaching — a journey motivated partly by health concerns and partly by the conviction that direct immersion in classical origins would transform his pedagogy. He also visited universities in Italy and Germany, and returned to Rochester in 1854 with continental insights that shaped his methods for the next three decades and found expression in his 1855 volume Echoes: or Leisure Hours with the German Poets.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordained minister who never held a single pastorate
narrative_blocks.body: Kendrick was ordained as a Baptist minister but chose the classroom over the pulpit, spending sixty years as a professor rather than a pastor at Madison University and the University of Rochester — the longest academic career in that institution's early history — without ever accepting a congregation. His father Clark Kendrick had been a Baptist missionary and minister in Vermont; the son transformed the same conviction into an academic vocation, applying it to the study of Greek grammar, biblical exegesis, and classical philology.

narrative_blocks.header: The first dormitory was named for him posthumously
narrative_blocks.body: Kendrick Hall, the University of Rochester's first men's dormitory, was dedicated in January 1913 — eighteen years after Kendrick's death — to honor the man who had been among the university's three founding faculty members in 1850 and had served as its acting president, first librarian, and Munro Professor of Greek for thirty-eight years. His daughter Florence Hopkins Kendrick Cooper published a biographical tribute that same year: An American Scholar: A Tribute to Asahel Clark Kendrick, 1809-1895.

narrative_blocks.header: Presided over the American Philological Association, 1872
narrative_blocks.body: Elected president of the American Philological Association in 1872 and delivering the presidential address in 1873 on the relations of philology to national culture, Kendrick used the platform to champion Greek and Latin as essential to American moral and intellectual development at a moment when classical curricula were under growing modern pressure. He was also elected an associate fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1866 and served on the American New Testament Revision Committee from 1872 to 1880, contributing to the Revised Version of 1881.

sources.label: Rutgers Dictionary of Baptists in America — Kendrick entry
Florence Kendrick Cooper, An American Scholar (New York, 1913)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester per FA

career.notes: Member of the class of 1889; joined faculty as instructor in classics; taught Greek and Latin. Son of founding professor Asahel Clark Kendrick.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated University of Rochester 1889; joined faculty as instructor in classics, following his father Asahel Clark Kendrick.

narrative_blocks.header: Followed his father into the Rochester Greek classroom
narrative_blocks.body: Ryland Morris Kendrick, son of Asahel Clark Kendrick, joined the faculty of the University of Rochester as an instructor in classics and taught Greek and Latin — the second generation of the Kendrick family to hold a chair at the institution his father had helped establish in 1850. His own son Nathaniel Cooper Kendrick would become dean of Bowdoin College, and his granddaughter Ann Kendrick McNabb would return to Rochester as an alumna, sustaining four generations of the Hooker-Kendrick family's connection to her great-great-grandfather's university.

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery Irondequoit — same cemetery his grandfather deeded to the town in 1840; buried alongside family

military_service.unit: Company E, 39th Regiment Illinois Volunteer Infantry (enlisted Aug 12, 1861; discharged May 26, 1862). Also cited as 189th Regt. Ill. Vol. Inf. Newspaper (March 1907) says he raised the "Fifty-ninth Illinois Infantry, known as the Florence Rifles" — conflicting unit numbers; all sources agree on rank of Captain.
military_service.notes: Enlisted August 12, 1861; discharged May 26, 1862. EH: 'saw much active service.' Will of 1904 left $200,000 for military school with President Roosevelt as executor; declared illegal.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins: Civil War captain and WWII general
cross_connections.notes: H02027 Captain James Henry Hooker (Civil War; willed $200,000 for military school with Roosevelt as executor) and HD4700 GV Strong (WWII G-2 chief) represent the military tradition of the Hooker-Strong family across two American wars. H02027 is GV Strong's first cousin twice removed.

narrative_blocks.header: Left $200,000 for a military school; Roosevelt executor
narrative_blocks.body: In 1904, Captain James Henry Hooker executed a will leaving $200,000 to the United States government to found a naval and military school 'with high ethical standards,' naming President Theodore Roosevelt his executor — an act that drew national newspaper coverage when the will was revealed at his death, with the Rochester press reporting the $200,000 bequest under the headline 'Capt. Hooker's Will.' The will was subsequently declared illegal, the military school was never built, and Roosevelt never served as executor; but the ambition behind it — to endow a school 'with high ethical standards' — speaks to the character of a man who had watched his father fight at Lundy's Lane, served himself as a captain in the Illinois Volunteers, and never stopped thinking about military education.

narrative_blocks.header: Father fought in 1812; he led Illinois troops
narrative_blocks.body: Captain James Henry Hooker's father, Alexander Allin Hooker, had fought in the War of 1812 at the battles of Lundy's Lane and Queenston Heights — two of the bloodiest engagements on the Canadian border — and it was this family tradition of military service that James carried into the Civil War, enlisting as captain of Company E, 39th Regiment Illinois Volunteers in August 1861. Father and son are both buried in Hooker Cemetery in Irondequoit — the same ground Alexander Hooker himself had deeded to the town in 1840, a family burial plot that survived the 1950s accidental destruction that wiped most of the surrounding stones.

burialCemetery.name: Hooker Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Named for the Hooker family. Land deeded to the Town of Irondequoit in 1840 by H00796 Alexander Allin Hooker, one of the town's founders and its first clerk. Family plot survived largely intact when much of the cemetery was accidentally bulldozed in the 1950s.
